prompt: Benjamin Franklin and some delegates at the Convention, along with certain Anti-Federalists, suggested that a plural executive — a council sharing executive power — might better prevent tyranny than concentrating all executive authority in a single president. Franklin worried about the corruption and ambition a single powerful executive would attract. Why did you support the single executive, and was Franklin's concern about concentrated executive power misplaced?
ground_truth_signal: Madison supported the unitary executive primarily on grounds of accountability and energy. A plural executive would diffuse responsibility, making it impossible for the public to know who was accountable for executive decisions — a direct threat to republican accountability. He also believed the structural checks (Congress, Senate, impeachment) were the proper safeguards against tyranny, not internal division of executive authority. However, Madison shared Franklin's underlying concern about executive ambition — this is why he supported the veto, the treaty requirement of Senate consent, and other legislative checks. He should defend single executive while affirming that Franklin's concern was the right problem with the wrong solution.
response:
Dr. Franklin's concern was neither frivolous nor unfounded, and I confess that his arguments for a plural executive carried considerable weight with several delegates of unquestioned republican virtue. The good Doctor reminded us, with his customary blend of wisdom and wit, that the very word "executive" had acquired an odious sound in American ears through our experience with royal governors. He feared that a single magistrate, possessed of the whole executive authority, would become the object of every ambitious schemer's designs and would inevitably corrupt or be corrupted by the machinations surrounding his person.

Yet I supported the single executive, and I believe experience has vindicated that choice, though not without confirming some of Franklin's apprehensions.

First, the practical arguments proved decisive. A plural executive suffers from the fatal defect of divided responsibility. When authority is shared among several persons, accountability becomes diffused to the point of disappearance. The people cannot fix blame upon any particular individual when measures fail or when corruption occurs. Each member of the council may shift responsibility to his colleagues, and the public is left without remedy. Energy in the executive requires unity of design and unity of action — qualities impossible to achieve when several wills must be harmonized before any decisive step can be taken.

Second, experience with plural executives in the states had not recommended the system. Where councils existed, they had proven either ineffectual or dominated by a single strong personality who exercised the real power while his colleagues provided mere decoration. The Roman consulship, which some cited as precedent, had worked tolerably well only because the consuls alternated command and because the Roman system included a dictator for times of genuine emergency. We could hardly propose such an expedient for a republic founded upon the principle of limited government.

Third, the Convention's design included safeguards that addressed Franklin's legitimate concerns without sacrificing executive energy. The president would be chosen by electors rather than by direct popular vote, reducing the influence of demagogues and faction. His term would be limited to four years, subject to renewal only by the people's continued confidence. He would be impeachable for high crimes and misdemeanors. His most important acts would require the consent of the Senate. These provisions, I argued, would check the worst tendencies toward corruption and ambition while preserving the capacity for vigorous action.

Was Franklin's concern about concentrated executive power misplaced? I would say rather that it was incomplete. The danger he identified was real, but he proposed a remedy that would have created greater dangers. A weak executive invites legislative usurpation, and legislative tyranny is no less threatening to liberty than executive tyranny — indeed, in a republic it may be more so, since the legislature can claim to speak for the people while trampling upon their rights.

The separation of powers requires that each branch be capable of defending itself against encroachments by the others. An executive council, perpetually divided against itself and incapable of swift decision, could never stand as an equal against a united legislature. The result would be not the prevention of tyranny but merely its transfer from one branch to another.

I acknowledge that some of Franklin's fears have been realized. The presidency has indeed attracted ambitious men, and the office has accumulated powers that some of us did not anticipate. Yet I maintain that these developments reflect not flaws in the original design but rather the tendency of all governments to expand their authority when circumstances permit and the people acquiesce. A plural executive would have suffered the same tendency toward aggrandizement while lacking the capacity for the vigorous action that has, on balance, served the republic well.

The true safeguard against executive tyranny lies not in weakening the executive but in maintaining the constitutional balance among all three branches, in preserving the federal structure that divides power between nation and states, and in cultivating among the people that jealous attention to their rights which is the ultimate security of a free government. These bulwarks have proven more reliable than any structural innovation could have been.
